title: Anisotropic Moiré Fractional Chern Insulators and Their Phase Transitions
abstract: Recently, moiré heterostructures have been observed to host fractional Chern insulator (FCI) phases at zero magnetic field. In this work, we show that the FCI phases are robust against moderate lattice anisotropy, while sufficiently strong anisotropy drives quantum phase transitions from incompressible topological phases to competing charge-density-wave (CDW) and Fermi-liquid (FL) phases. Specifically, in the case of twisted transition metal dichalcogenides (such as MoTe$_2$), the anisotropy arises from an interlayer momentum shift induced by heterostrain. We find that increasing anisotropy suppresses the many-body topological gap, eventually destabilizing the fractional topological phase. Beyond a critical anisotropy value, the system gradually transitions to a symmetry-broken state characterized by stripe-like CDW order. It has a Landau Level counterpart, where effective mass anisotropy provides an additional tuning knob for the fractional quantum Hall state; sufficiently strong anisotropy drives a transition to a charge-ordered phase. Moreover, in the anisotropic ideal Chern band model with lattice stretching, a Fermi liquid phase emerges as anisotropy increases. Our results establish that anisotropy provides a direct route for engineering and exploring competing correlated phases in FCI states based on moiré materials.
summary: This work explores how lattice anisotropy destabilizes Fractional Chern Insulator (FCI) phases in moiré materials. By tuning anisotropy, the system undergoes quantum phase transitions from a topological insulator to competing charge-density-wave or Fermi-liquid states. This establishes anisotropy as a powerful tool for engineering and exploring diverse correlated phases in moiré heterostructures.
main_problem: The study investigates the stability and nature of Fractional Chern Insulator (FCI) phases in moiré heterostructures when subjected to varying degrees of lattice anisotropy.
main_result: Sufficiently strong lattice anisotropy drives quantum phase transitions, destabilizing the incompressible FCI state into competing charge-density-wave (CDW) or Fermi-liquid (FL) phases.
method: The research employs theoretical modeling using continuum Hamiltonians and analyzing key many-body observables like the structure factor and topological gap.
model_or_system: The system is based on moiré heterostructures, such as twisted TMDs ($	ext{MoTe}_2$), modeled via a continuum Hamiltonian incorporating interlayer tunneling and electron-electron interactions.
key_observables: Hall conductivity ($\sigma_{xy}$), topological gap size, structure factor $S(\mathbf{q})$, and Bloch state occupation numbers ($n_k$).
important_parameters: Lattice anisotropy, controlled by interlayer momentum shift ($\gamma_q b(t)$) induced by heterostrain, or lattice stretching ($ eta$).
main_assumptions: The analysis often utilizes the single Landau Level (LL) approximation and treats the interaction via projection onto the Chern band.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the phase transition from FCI (topological gap) to CDW/FL as anisotropy increases, showing changes in $S(\mathbf{q})$ and $n_k$.
paper_structure: The paper systematically analyzes the effect of anisotropy on the FCI state, first showing robustness to moderate strain, then detailing the transition mechanism to CDW/FL states, and finally comparing these findings to anisotropic FQHE models.
why_interesting: The mechanism of anisotropy-driven phase transitions from topological to symmetry-broken/compressible states provides a general framework for engineering correlated phases in moiré systems, relevant to understanding emergent quantum states in artificial lattices.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic picture of moir´e heterostructure formed by twisted bilayer honeycomb lattice such as graphene or tran- sition metal dichalcogenides. (b) When anisotropy is intro- duced, there is the phase transition from incompressible phase (FCI) with topological gap to compressible phase (CDW or Fermi liquid).

FIG. 2. Dispersion of the single-particle Hamiltonian, spec- tra of the screened Coulomb interaction projected onto the Chern band, Structure factor, and occupation number of ground states. (a,b) Energy dispersion of Eq. (1) without and with internal magnetic field modulation, corresponding to γ = 0 and γ = 0.3, respectively. (c,d) Spectra of the screened Coulomb interaction projected onto the Chern band for (γ = 0) and γ = 0.3, respectively. (e,f) Structure factor of the ground states under the projected screened Coulomb interaction for γ = 0 and γ = 0.3, respectively. (g,h) Orbital occupation number of the ground state for γ = 0 and γ = 0.3, respectively.

FIG. 3. (a) The schematic of stretching the lattice to in- duce anisotropy. (b) FCI gap and ground state bandwidth as functions of lattice ratio β. (c,d) The energy spectra of the Coulomb interaction on the ideal flat band corresponding to β = 1 and β = 0.5, respectively. (e,f) Structure factor of the ground states under the Coulomb interaction projected onto the ideal flat band corresponding to a regular honeycomb lat- tice (β = 1) and a stretched lattice (β = 0.5), respectively. (g,h) Orbital occupation number of the ground state for β = 1 and β = 0.5, respectively.

FIG. 4. The spectra of Coulomb interaction on the first Landau Level at filling ν = 1/2 for (a) gi = δab with α = 1 (green dots) and α = 0.92 (magenta dots). (b) gi = gm with α = 1 (green dots) and α = 0.6 (magenta dots). The α = 1 corresponds to the isotropic case, giving the sixfold Moore- Read states in the red boxes.
